pageTitle.value: Menkab - Revealing hidden treasures - Perpetual Planet

pageCaption.value: Not far from the populated coastline of northern Italy, the seas teem with cetaceans such as sperm whales and fin whales. With the support of Rolex’s Perpetual Planet Initiative, members of marine research group Menkab are monitoring populations and working to raise awareness of the creatures’ habitat.

metadescription.value: With the support of Rolex, marine research group Menkab is working to raise awareness of cetacean species in the Ligurian Sea. More on rolex.org.

text.value: Not far from the populated coastline of northern Italy, the seas teem with cetaceans such as sperm whales and fin whales. With the support of Rolex’s Perpetual Planet Initiative, members of marine research group Menkab are monitoring populations and working to raise awareness of the creatures’ habitat.

The port of Savona on the west coast of northern Italy is heavily built-up. Formerly a major centre for the iron industry, today it heaves with tourists thanks to its spectacular churches and fortresses. But if you head a little way out to sea, you can see sperm whales, one of the largest animals alive.

In the oceans, sperm whales routinely can grow to 16 metres in length, whereas those in the Mediterranean Sea do not exceed 13 m. Their size and power mean they are among the most spectacular animals to see in the wild, but can be elusive, diving to depths of more than 1 kilometre in search of prey such as squid.

Despite the intensive human presence in the region, many sperm whales choose to spend their time in the Ligurian Sea, an arm of the Mediterranean indenting the north-western coast of Italy. “Cetaceans are really very intelligent animals with great adaptive abilities,” says Giulia Calogero, president of ocean conservation organization Menkab. That adaptability enables them to live alongside humankind.

Nevertheless, it is essential for the world to understand how they live and the threats they face in order to protect them. It is also crucial to raise awareness of them. Menkab is doing both, with the support of Rolex’s Perpetual Planet Initiative. The Arabic word “Menkab” is the name of a star in the constellation Cetus, or “the whale”. “It is the star that represents the blowhole,” says Calogero. The organization’s founder, Maurizio Würtz, chose the name because it signifies a place where cetaceans go to breathe, to be safe and to thrive in their natural environment.
The organization’s founder, Maurizio Würtz, chose the name [Menkab] because it signifies a place where cetaceans go to breathe, to be safe and to thrive in their natural environment.

When Biagio Violi first went to sea with Menkab in 2013, he saw sperm whales within the first 10 minutes. “I was completely astonished, wordless,” he recalls. “I was really impressed by the size and beauty of the animal, the elegance of its movements.” Swiftly deciding to dedicate his career to studying and protecting sperm whales, he is now Menkab’s scientific co-ordinator.

The team’s work focuses on the Pelagos Sanctuary, a protected area spanning 87,500 square kilometres in the Ligurian Sea. It is home to eight cetacean species. Fin whales, the second-largest animals alive behind blue whales, use it as a breeding ground. The sanctuary is also home to several species of dolphin, including striped and bottlenose dolphins. Considering that the Mediterranean Sea is enclosed and relatively small, “it actually also has a very high diversity of marine mammals, and cetaceans in particular,” says Calogero.

One of Menkab’s ongoing projects is to survey the cetacean species living in the waters of the Pelagos Sanctuary. Teams go out to sea every season in rigid inflatable boats (RIBs) to see the animals surfacing. Most of the surveys are performed from the northern hemisphere spring to early autumn (March to October), but some are carried out in winter when the weather conditions are good. Whenever possible, team members photograph and identify individual animals using the distinctive marks on their bodies. “Sometimes the marks are so inconspicuous, they are small speckles… maybe some pigmented areas. It is not at all easy to do,” says Calogero.

Disturbingly, the most recognizable animals are often those that have been struck by boat propellers, which leave large and visible marks. For example, the team has been monitoring a sperm whale they call Atlante for two years. As Calogero describes: “It has a very easy-to-identify tail, but, of course, it is not good for the animal itself.” Ship strikes are one of the main threats to sperm whales in this region, alongside entanglement in fishing nets and ingesting plastic waste.

By tracking individual animals, and sharing this information with research organizations internationally, Menkab is able to monitor not only the health and populations of each species but also the vast distances they travel. Whales known to Menkab have been spotted by colleagues in Israel, Iceland and the West Indies.

This was a key factor in Rolex’s decision to support the organization. For nearly a century, the brand has supported pioneering explorers pushing back the boundaries of human endeavour, moving from championing exploration for the sake of discovery to that which seeks to protect and preserve the planet. This engagement was reinforced with the launch of the Perpetual Planet Initiative in 2019.

The other pillar of Menkab’s work consists of public engagement and raising awareness; of both the presence of cetaceans in the area and the need to protect them.

“We prefer not to simply deliver theoretical lectures to [teenage] students,” says Violi. “We bring them out to sea so they can observe, and start loving nature, and of their own accord take action to protect our sea life.”

We prefer not to simply deliver theoretical lectures to [teenage] students. We bring them out to sea so they can observe, and start loving nature, and of their own accord take action to protect our sea life.

Adds Calogero: “When we tell people that only a short distance from the coast they are able to see fin whales or sperm whales, they are often surprised. Our goal is to try to bring this message as much as possible to the general public and to students.”

A key element of this is Menkab’s remotely operated vehicle (ROV), an underwater machine equipped with a camera that can be controlled from the water surface. Although primarily used for research – for example to film deep areas that are difficult to reach by diving – it is increasingly being deployed to introduce students to diving and the underwater world.

For the students, seeing the marine environment via the ROV becomes almost like a video game, says Calogero, making the opportunity to drive it even more exciting. “They can literally test themselves,” she says. “Students learn how to do what can then basically be a job in the future, but they can experience it in a way [that is] almost playful.”

Many of the students become so passionate about the sea that they enrol in marine biology courses. “We need to keep teaching and learning and inspiring people,” says Violi. In this way, he hopes Menkab can help drive a generational shift in how people treat the ocean.

pageTitle.value: One Ocean Foundation and Caprera Canyon - Perpetual Planet

pageCaption.value: Through its Perpetual Planet Initiative, Rolex supports One Ocean Foundation which surveys whales and other animals that thrive in the waters of the Mediterranean’s Caprera Canyon in order to better protect them.

metadescription.value: With the support of Rolex, One Ocean Foundation is aiming to secure protection for the Caprera Canyon and its diverse wildlife. More on rolex.org.

pageTitle.value: Rolex Awards - Listening to the Earth

pageCaption.value: Since designing an underwater system to prevent ships from colliding with whales, bioacoustics pioneer Michel André has expanded his project to create a network of microphones that monitor the planet's myriad sounds and warn of threats to nature.

WaPageTitle.value: Rolex Awards - Listening to the Earth

metadescription.value: Michel André’s bioacoustics work has led to a growing network of underwater microphones that aims to protect marine life. Learn more on rolex.org.

pageTitle.value: Rolex and Mission Blue - The Galápagos Islands Hope Spot

pageCaption.value: Through its Perpetual Planet Initiative, Rolex is supporting ocean conservation non-profit Mission Blue in its goal to create a global network of Hope Spots, areas that deserve protection as they are home to vital marine ecosystems.

WaPageTitle.value: Rolex and Mission Blue - The Galápagos Islands Hope Spot

metadescription.value: The waters of the Galápagos Islands, named a Hope Spot by Mission Blue, are a unique living laboratory of diverse endemic species. More on rolex.org.
